The total area of the cluster spans approximately 2 kilometers north-south and 1.5 kilometers east-west<ref>Nagayama (1988) 11.401-402</ref>.
As a result of construction projects, such as the [[:en:Kinki Expressway|Kinki Expressway]], urban planning initiatives, and residential developments, archaeological surveys continued, identifying 213 tumuli by 2006<ref>Terai (2006) p.273</ref>.
== Formation Period == The Nagahara Kofun Cluster is believed to have formed over four periods, from the late 4th century to the early 6th century, based on the chronology of haniwa and [[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]] unearthed at the site<ref>Nagayama (1988) p.405</ref><ref name="takashima424">Takashima (1989) p.424</ref>. 1. '''Phase 1 (late 4th century)''': Larger tumuli, such as the Tsukano-moto Kofun and Ikka-zuka Kofun (scallop-shaped keyhole tomb), were constructed. 2. '''Phases 2-3 (5th century)''': Smaller tumuli, primarily square-shaped, were built surrounding the earlier large tumuli. 3. '''Phase 4 (early 6th century)''': Tumuli such as the Shichinotsubo Kofun and Minamiguchi Kofun were constructed, marking the end of activity at the site<ref>Nagayama (1988) 11.404-415</ref><ref name="#1">Terai (2006) 11.274-275</ref>.
The predominance of small square tumuli from the 5th century aligns the Nagahara Kofun Cluster with early cluster tumuli like the [[:en:Niizawa Senzuka Kofun Cluster|Niizawa Senzuka Kofun Cluster]] in Nara. Furthermore, its contemporaneity with the [[:en:Furuichi Kofun Cluster|Furuichi Kofun Cluster]], one of Japan's largest kofun clusters, suggests a possible connection. The evolution from larger Phase 1 tumuli to smaller Phase 2-3 tumuli indicates a shift from indirect regional chieftain rule to direct authority by the {{ill|Yamato kingship|ja|大王権|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato kingship|qq=}}<ref name="#2">Ōba (2006) p.1</ref>. Some scholars propose that the buried individuals were primitive bureaucrats integrated into Yamato's administrative system<ref>Miki (2011) p.239</ref>.

These primarily consist of {{ill|Wooden Coffin|lt=Wooden Coffin|lt=Wooden coffin burials|ja|木棺直葬|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/wooden coffin burials|qq=}} or large cylindrical haniwa repurposed as coffins, with a single example of a clay sarcophagus. The Shichinotsubo Kofun (Tomb 130) features the foundation of an early corridor-style stone chamber, rare for the cluster<ref name="#1"/>.
Artifacts discovered include iron swords, axes, sickles, Sue pottery, [[:en:Haji pottery|Haji pottery]], beads, and horse fittings. Numerous haniwa have also been unearthed, including cylindrical, jar-shaped, and figurative types (e.g., human, horse, armor, shield, roof, and boat-shaped haniwa). Notably, boat-shaped haniwa from Tombs 1 and 2 of the Takamawari subgroup have been designated Important Cultural Properties by the Japanese government<ref>Kyojima (2007) p.29</ref><ref>Monthly Cultural Properties, 1992, 11.17-20, 42</ref>.
== Designated Cultural Properties == === Important Cultural Properties (National) === * [[:en:Haniwa|Haniwa]] from the Nagahara Takamawari Kofun Group, including: ''' Tomb 1: house-shaped, boat-shaped, cylindrical haniwa, and more. ''' Tomb 2: house-shaped, boat-shaped, shield-shaped, and armor-shaped haniwa.

'''Nagahara Kofun Cluster''' (長原古墳群) refers to a group of [[late Kofun period]] tumuli located beneath Nagahara and its surrounding areas in [[:en:Hirano-ku, Osaka|Hirano-ku]], [[:en:Osaka|Osaka City]], [[Osaka Prefecture]]. This cluster, which dates from the late 4th to early 6th centuries, was discovered during the extension of the {{ill|Osaka Municipal Subway Tanimachi Line|ja|大阪市営地下鉄谷町線|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Osaka Municipal Subway Tanimachi Line|qq=}} and is known for the excavation of over 200 tumuli and numerous [[:en:Haniwa|haniwa]].
== Discovery and Investigation of the Nagahara Kofun Cluster == The discovery of the Nagahara Kofun Cluster occurred in 1973, during an excavation survey prompted by the extension of the Osaka Municipal Subway Tanimachi Line. Although there were no visible surface traces of tumuli, cylindrical haniwa began to emerge, leading to further investigation. Subsequent surveys revealed numerous small tumuli remnants with circumferential moats, despite much of their mound soil having been lost<ref>Nagayama (1988) 11.400-401</ref>.
Among the discoveries was a large circular mound over 50 meters in diameter, with remnants indicating a two-tiered structure. This tumulus, located in an area historically called "Tsukano-moto," was named the Tsukano-moto Kofun. Further examination of cadastral maps uncovered place names such as Ikka-zuka, Ochizuka, Kitsunezuka, and Takazuka, indicating the possible past presence of tumuli. The total area of the cluster spans approximately 2 kilometers north-south and 1.5 kilometers east-west<ref>Nagayama (1988) 11.401-402</ref>.
As a result of construction projects, such as the [[:en:Kinki Expressway|Kinki Expressway]], urban planning initiatives, and residential developments, archaeological surveys continued, identifying 213 tumuli by 2006<ref>Terai (2006) p.273</ref>.
== Formation Period == The Nagahara Kofun Cluster is believed to have formed over four periods, from the late 4th century to the early 6th century, based on the chronology of haniwa and [[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]] unearthed at the site<ref>Nagayama (1988) p.405</ref><ref name="takashima424">Takashima (1989) p.424</ref>. 1. '''Phase 1 (late 4th century)''': Larger tumuli, such as the Tsukano-moto Kofun and Ikka-zuka Kofun (scallop-shaped keyhole tomb), were constructed. 2. '''Phases 2-3 (5th century)''': Smaller tumuli, primarily square-shaped, were built surrounding the earlier large tumuli. 3. '''Phase 4 (early 6th century)''': Tumuli such as the Shichinotsubo Kofun and Minamiguchi Kofun were constructed, marking the end of activity at the site<ref>Nagayama (1988) 11.404-415</ref><ref name="#1">Terai (2006) 11.274-275</ref>.
The predominance of small square tumuli from the 5th century aligns the Nagahara Kofun Cluster with early cluster tumuli like the [[:en:Niizawa Senzuka Kofun Cluster|Niizawa Senzuka Kofun Cluster]] in Nara. Furthermore, its contemporaneity with the [[:en:Furuichi Kofun Cluster|Furuichi Kofun Cluster]], one of Japan's largest kofun clusters, suggests a possible connection. The evolution from larger Phase 1 tumuli to smaller Phase 2-3 tumuli indicates a shift from indirect regional chieftain rule to direct authority by the {{ill|Yamato kingship|ja|大王権|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato kingship|qq=}}<ref name="#2">Ōba (2006) p.1</ref>. Some scholars propose that the buried individuals were primitive bureaucrats integrated into Yamato's administrative system<ref>Miki (2011) p.239</ref>.
== Internal Structure and Artifacts == Due to extensive later flattening, intact burial facilities are rare, with only 18–19 examples surviving. These primarily consist of {{ill|Wooden Coffin|lt=Wooden Coffin|lt=Wooden coffin burials|ja|木棺直葬|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/wooden coffin burials|qq=}} or large cylindrical haniwa repurposed as coffins, with a single example of a clay sarcophagus. The Shichinotsubo Kofun (Tomb 130) features the foundation of an early corridor-style stone chamber, rare for the cluster<ref name="#1"/>.
Artifacts discovered include iron swords, axes, sickles, Sue pottery, [[:en:Haji pottery|Haji pottery]], beads, and horse fittings. Numerous haniwa have also been unearthed, including cylindrical, jar-shaped, and figurative types (e.g., human, horse, armor, shield, roof, and boat-shaped haniwa). Notably, boat-shaped haniwa from Tombs 1 and 2 of the Takamawari subgroup have been designated Important Cultural Properties by the Japanese government<ref>Kyojima (2007) p.29</ref><ref>Monthly Cultural Properties, 1992, 11.17-20, 42</ref>.
== Designated Cultural Properties == === Important Cultural Properties (National) === * [[:en:Haniwa|Haniwa]] from the Nagahara Takamawari Kofun Group, including: ''' Tomb 1: house-shaped, boat-shaped, cylindrical haniwa, and more. ''' Tomb 2: house-shaped, boat-shaped, shield-shaped, and armor-shaped haniwa.
=== Osaka City Designated Cultural Properties === * Shield-shaped haniwa from Tomb 40. * Burial goods from Shichinotsubo Kofun.
== References == {{Reflist|2}}
== Bibliography == * Nagayama Masakazu, "Nagahara and Kami Kofun Clusters," in *New History of Osaka City, Vol. 1*, 1988. * Takashima Tetsu, "Nagahara Kofun Cluster," in *Comprehensive Dictionary of Japanese Kofun*, Tokyo Publishing, 1989. * Terai Makoto, *Nagahara Ruins Excavation Report XV*, Osaka Cultural Properties Association, 2006. == See Also == * {{ill|Misono Kofun|ja|美園古墳|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Misono Kofun|qq=}}
== External Links == * [http://www.occpa.or.jp/ikou/haniwa_gallery/ikou_01_01.html#gallery_05 Nagahara Takamawari Haniwa Gallery (Osaka Cultural Properties Research Institute)] * [https://www.city.osaka.lg.jp/kyoiku/page/0000074306.html Shichinotsubo Kofun Burial Goods (Osaka City)]
長原古墳群]]

revision.text:
'''Nagahara Kofun Cluster''' (長原古墳群) refers to a group of [[late Kofun period]] tumuli located beneath Nagahara and its surrounding areas in [[:en:Hirano-ku, Osaka|Hirano-ku]], [[:en:Osaka|Osaka City]], [[Osaka Prefecture]]. This cluster, which dates from the late 4th to early 6th centuries, was discovered during the extension of the {{ill|Osaka Municipal Subway Tanimachi Line|ja|大阪市営地下鉄谷町線|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Osaka Municipal Subway Tanimachi Line|qq=}} and is known for the excavation of over 200 tumuli and numerous [[:en:Haniwa|haniwa]].
== Discovery and Investigation of the Nagahara Kofun Cluster == The discovery of the Nagahara Kofun Cluster occurred in 1973, during an excavation survey prompted by the extension of the Osaka Municipal Subway Tanimachi Line. Although there were no visible surface traces of tumuli, cylindrical haniwa began to emerge, leading to further investigation. Subsequent surveys revealed numerous small tumuli remnants with circumferential moats, despite much of their mound soil having been lost<ref>Nagayama (1988) 11.400-401</ref>.
Among the discoveries was a large circular mound over 50 meters in diameter, with remnants indicating a two-tiered structure. This tumulus, located in an area historically called "Tsukano-moto," was named the Tsukano-moto Kofun. Further examination of cadastral maps uncovered place names such as Ikka-zuka, Ochizuka, Kitsunezuka, and Takazuka, indicating the possible past presence of tumuli. The total area of the cluster spans approximately 2 kilometers north-south and 1.5 kilometers east-west<ref>Nagayama (1988) 11.401-402</ref>.
As a result of construction projects, such as the [[:en:Kinki Expressway|Kinki Expressway]], urban planning initiatives, and residential developments, archaeological surveys continued, identifying 213 tumuli by 2006<ref>Terai (2006) p.273</ref>.
== Formation Period == The Nagahara Kofun Cluster is believed to have formed over four periods, from the late 4th century to the early 6th century, based on the chronology of haniwa and [[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]] unearthed at the site<ref>Nagayama (1988) p.405</ref><ref name="takashima424">Takashima (1989) p.424</ref>. 1. '''Phase 1 (late 4th century)''': Larger tumuli, such as the Tsukano-moto Kofun and Ikka-zuka Kofun (scallop-shaped keyhole tomb), were constructed. 2. '''Phases 2-3 (5th century)''': Smaller tumuli, primarily square-shaped, were built surrounding the earlier large tumuli. 3. '''Phase 4 (early 6th century)''': Tumuli such as the Shichinotsubo Kofun and Minamiguchi Kofun were constructed, marking the end of activity at the site<ref>Nagayama (1988) 11.404-415</ref><ref name="#1">Terai (2006) 11.274-275</ref>.
The predominance of small square tumuli from the 5th century aligns the Nagahara Kofun Cluster with early cluster tumuli like the [[:en:Niizawa Senzuka Kofun Cluster|Niizawa Senzuka Kofun Cluster]] in Nara. Furthermore, its contemporaneity with the [[:en:Furuichi Kofun Cluster|Furuichi Kofun Cluster]], one of Japan's largest kofun clusters, suggests a possible connection. The evolution from larger Phase 1 tumuli to smaller Phase 2-3 tumuli indicates a shift from indirect regional chieftain rule to direct authority by the {{ill|Yamato kingship|ja|大王権|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato kingship|qq=}}<ref name="#2">Ōba (2006) p.1</ref>. Some scholars propose that the buried individuals were primitive bureaucrats integrated into Yamato's administrative system<ref>Miki (2011) p.239</ref>.
== Internal Structure and Artifacts == Due to extensive later flattening, intact burial facilities are rare, with only 18–19 examples surviving. These primarily consist of {{ill|Wooden Coffin|lt=Wooden Coffin|lt=Wooden coffin burials|ja|木棺直葬|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/wooden coffin burials|qq=}} or large cylindrical haniwa repurposed as coffins, with a single example of a clay sarcophagus. The Shichinotsubo Kofun (Tomb 130) features the foundation of an early corridor-style stone chamber, rare for the cluster<ref name="#1"/>.
Artifacts discovered include iron swords, axes, sickles, Sue pottery, [[:en:Haji pottery|Haji pottery]], beads, and horse fittings. Numerous haniwa have also been unearthed, including cylindrical, jar-shaped, and figurative types (e.g., human, horse, armor, shield, roof, and boat-shaped haniwa). Notably, boat-shaped haniwa from Tombs 1 and 2 of the Takamawari subgroup have been designated Important Cultural Properties by the Japanese government<ref>Kyojima (2007) p.29</ref><ref>Monthly Cultural Properties, 1992, 11.17-20, 42</ref>.
== Designated Cultural Properties == === Important Cultural Properties (National) === * [[:en:Haniwa|Haniwa]] from the Nagahara Takamawari Kofun Group, including: ''' Tomb 1: house-shaped, boat-shaped, cylindrical haniwa, and more. ''' Tomb 2: house-shaped, boat-shaped, shield-shaped, and armor-shaped haniwa.
=== Osaka City Designated Cultural Properties === * Shield-shaped haniwa from Tomb 40. * Burial goods from Shichinotsubo Kofun.
== References == {{Reflist|2}}
== Bibliography == * Nagayama Masakazu, "Nagahara and Kami Kofun Clusters," in *New History of Osaka City, Vol. 1*, 1988. * Takashima Tetsu, "Nagahara Kofun Cluster," in *Comprehensive Dictionary of Japanese Kofun*, Tokyo Publishing, 1989. * Terai Makoto, *Nagahara Ruins Excavation Report XV*, Osaka Cultural Properties Association, 2006. == See Also == * {{ill|Misono Kofun|ja|美園古墳|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Misono Kofun|qq=}}
== External Links == * [http://www.occpa.or.jp/ikou/haniwa_gallery/ikou_01_01.html#gallery_05 Nagahara Takamawari Haniwa Gallery (Osaka Cultural Properties Research Institute)] * [https://www.city.osaka.lg.jp/kyoiku/page/0000074306.html Shichinotsubo Kofun Burial Goods (Osaka City)]
長原古墳群]]
{{translated page|ja|長原古墳群|version=100954068|comment=Imported full ja history}}

revision.comment: Bot: remove deleted category Cluster tumuli
revision.text:
'''Nagahara Kofun Cluster''' (長原古墳群) refers to a group of [[late Kofun period]] tumuli located beneath Nagahara and its surrounding areas in [[:en:Hirano-ku, Osaka|Hirano-ku]], [[:en:Osaka|Osaka City]], [[Osaka Prefecture]]. This cluster, which dates from the late 4th to early 6th centuries, was discovered during the extension of the {{ill|Osaka Municipal Subway Tanimachi Line|ja|大阪市営地下鉄谷町線|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Osaka Municipal Subway Tanimachi Line|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to Osaka Metro谷町線}} and is known for the excavation of over 200 tumuli and numerous [[:en:Haniwa|haniwa]].
== Discovery and Investigation of the Nagahara Kofun Cluster == The discovery of the Nagahara Kofun Cluster occurred in 1973, during an excavation survey prompted by the extension of the Osaka Municipal Subway Tanimachi Line. Although there were no visible surface traces of tumuli, cylindrical haniwa began to emerge, leading to further investigation. Subsequent surveys revealed numerous small tumuli remnants with circumferential moats, despite much of their mound soil having been lost<ref>Nagayama (1988) 11.400-401</ref>.
Among the discoveries was a large circular mound over 50 meters in diameter, with remnants indicating a two-tiered structure. This tumulus, located in an area historically called "Tsukano-moto," was named the Tsukano-moto Kofun. Further examination of cadastral maps uncovered place names such as Ikka-zuka, Ochizuka, Kitsunezuka, and Takazuka, indicating the possible past presence of tumuli. The total area of the cluster spans approximately 2 kilometers north-south and 1.5 kilometers east-west<ref>Nagayama (1988) 11.401-402</ref>.
As a result of construction projects, such as the [[:en:Kinki Expressway|Kinki Expressway]], urban planning initiatives, and residential developments, archaeological surveys continued, identifying 213 tumuli by 2006<ref>Terai (2006) p.273</ref>.
== Formation Period == The Nagahara Kofun Cluster is believed to have formed over four periods, from the late 4th century to the early 6th century, based on the chronology of haniwa and [[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]] unearthed at the site<ref>Nagayama (1988) p.405</ref><ref name="takashima424">Takashima (1989) p.424</ref>. 1. '''Phase 1 (late 4th century)''': Larger tumuli, such as the Tsukano-moto Kofun and Ikka-zuka Kofun (scallop-shaped keyhole tomb), were constructed. 2. '''Phases 2-3 (5th century)''': Smaller tumuli, primarily square-shaped, were built surrounding the earlier large tumuli. 3. '''Phase 4 (early 6th century)''': Tumuli such as the Shichinotsubo Kofun and Minamiguchi Kofun were constructed, marking the end of activity at the site<ref>Nagayama (1988) 11.404-415</ref><ref name="#1">Terai (2006) 11.274-275</ref>.
The predominance of small square tumuli from the 5th century aligns the Nagahara Kofun Cluster with early cluster tumuli like the [[:en:Niizawa Senzuka Kofun Cluster|Niizawa Senzuka Kofun Cluster]] in Nara. Furthermore, its contemporaneity with the [[:en:Furuichi Kofun Cluster|Furuichi Kofun Cluster]], one of Japan's largest kofun clusters, suggests a possible connection. The evolution from larger Phase 1 tumuli to smaller Phase 2-3 tumuli indicates a shift from indirect regional chieftain rule to direct authority by the {{ill|Yamato kingship|ja|大王権|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato kingship|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}<ref name="#2">Ōba (2006) p.1</ref>. Some scholars propose that the buried individuals were primitive bureaucrats integrated into Yamato's administrative system<ref>Miki (2011) p.239</ref>.
== Internal Structure and Artifacts == Due to extensive later flattening, intact burial facilities are rare, with only 18–19 examples surviving. These primarily consist of {{ill|Wooden Coffin|lt=Wooden Coffin|lt=Wooden coffin burials|ja|木棺直葬|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/wooden coffin burials|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 古墳}} or large cylindrical haniwa repurposed as coffins, with a single example of a clay sarcophagus. The Shichinotsubo Kofun (Tomb 130) features the foundation of an early corridor-style stone chamber, rare for the cluster<ref name="#1"/>.
Artifacts discovered include iron swords, axes, sickles, Sue pottery, [[:en:Haji pottery|Haji pottery]], beads, and horse fittings. Numerous haniwa have also been unearthed, including cylindrical, jar-shaped, and figurative types (e.g., human, horse, armor, shield, roof, and boat-shaped haniwa). Notably, boat-shaped haniwa from Tombs 1 and 2 of the Takamawari subgroup have been designated Important Cultural Properties by the Japanese government<ref>Kyojima (2007) p.29</ref><ref>Monthly Cultural Properties, 1992, 11.17-20, 42</ref>.
== Designated Cultural Properties == === Important Cultural Properties (National) === * [[:en:Haniwa|Haniwa]] from the Nagahara Takamawari Kofun Group, including: ''' Tomb 1: house-shaped, boat-shaped, cylindrical haniwa, and more. ''' Tomb 2: house-shaped, boat-shaped, shield-shaped, and armor-shaped haniwa.
=== Osaka City Designated Cultural Properties === * Shield-shaped haniwa from Tomb 40. * Burial goods from Shichinotsubo Kofun.
== References == {{Reflist|2}}
== Bibliography == * Nagayama Masakazu, "Nagahara and Kami Kofun Clusters," in *New History of Osaka City, Vol. 1*, 1988. * Takashima Tetsu, "Nagahara Kofun Cluster," in *Comprehensive Dictionary of Japanese Kofun*, Tokyo Publishing, 1989. * Terai Makoto, *Nagahara Ruins Excavation Report XV*, Osaka Cultural Properties Association, 2006. == See Also == * {{ill|Misono Kofun|ja|美園古墳|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Misono Kofun|qq=}}
== External Links == * [http://www.occpa.or.jp/ikou/haniwa_gallery/ikou_01_01.html#gallery_05 Nagahara Takamawari Haniwa Gallery (Osaka Cultural Properties Research Institute)] * [https://www.city.osaka.lg.jp/kyoiku/page/0000074306.html Shichinotsubo Kofun Burial Goods (Osaka City)]
長原古墳群]]

revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
'''Nagahara Kofun Cluster''' (長原古墳群) refers to a group of [[late Kofun period]] tumuli located beneath Nagahara and its surrounding areas in [[:en:Hirano-ku, Osaka|Hirano-ku]], [[:en:Osaka|Osaka City]], [[:en:Osaka Prefecture|Osaka Prefecture]]. This cluster, which dates from the late 4th to early 6th centuries, was discovered during the extension of the {{ill|Osaka Municipal Subway Tanimachi Line|ja|大阪市営地下鉄谷町線|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Osaka Municipal Subway Tanimachi Line|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to Osaka Metro谷町線}} and is known for the excavation of over 200 tumuli and numerous [[:en:Haniwa|haniwa]].
== Discovery and Investigation of the Nagahara Kofun Cluster == The discovery of the Nagahara Kofun Cluster occurred in 1973, during an excavation survey prompted by the extension of the Osaka Municipal Subway Tanimachi Line. Although there were no visible surface traces of tumuli, cylindrical haniwa began to emerge, leading to further investigation. Subsequent surveys revealed numerous small tumuli remnants with circumferential moats, despite much of their mound soil having been lost<ref>Nagayama (1988) 11.400-401</ref>.
Among the discoveries was a large circular mound over 50 meters in diameter, with remnants indicating a two-tiered structure. This tumulus, located in an area historically called "Tsukano-moto," was named the Tsukano-moto Kofun. Further examination of cadastral maps uncovered place names such as Ikka-zuka, Ochizuka, Kitsunezuka, and Takazuka, indicating the possible past presence of tumuli. The total area of the cluster spans approximately 2 kilometers north-south and 1.5 kilometers east-west<ref>Nagayama (1988) 11.401-402</ref>.
As a result of construction projects, such as the [[:en:Kinki Expressway|Kinki Expressway]], urban planning initiatives, and residential developments, archaeological surveys continued, identifying 213 tumuli by 2006<ref>Terai (2006) p.273</ref>.
== Formation Period == The Nagahara Kofun Cluster is believed to have formed over four periods, from the late 4th century to the early 6th century, based on the chronology of haniwa and [[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]] unearthed at the site<ref>Nagayama (1988) p.405</ref><ref name="takashima424">Takashima (1989) p.424</ref>. 1. '''Phase 1 (late 4th century)''': Larger tumuli, such as the Tsukano-moto Kofun and Ikka-zuka Kofun (scallop-shaped keyhole tomb), were constructed. 2. '''Phases 2-3 (5th century)''': Smaller tumuli, primarily square-shaped, were built surrounding the earlier large tumuli. 3. '''Phase 4 (early 6th century)''': Tumuli such as the Shichinotsubo Kofun and Minamiguchi Kofun were constructed, marking the end of activity at the site<ref>Nagayama (1988) 11.404-415</ref><ref name="#1">Terai (2006) 11.274-275</ref>.
The predominance of small square tumuli from the 5th century aligns the Nagahara Kofun Cluster with early cluster tumuli like the [[:en:Niizawa Senzuka Kofun Cluster|Niizawa Senzuka Kofun Cluster]] in Nara. Furthermore, its contemporaneity with the [[:en:Furuichi Kofun Cluster|Furuichi Kofun Cluster]], one of Japan's largest kofun clusters, suggests a possible connection. The evolution from larger Phase 1 tumuli to smaller Phase 2-3 tumuli indicates a shift from indirect regional chieftain rule to direct authority by the {{ill|Yamato kingship|ja|大王権|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato kingship|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}<ref name="#2">Ōba (2006) p.1</ref>. Some scholars propose that the buried individuals were primitive bureaucrats integrated into Yamato's administrative system<ref>Miki (2011) p.239</ref>.
== Internal Structure and Artifacts == Due to extensive later flattening, intact burial facilities are rare, with only 18–19 examples surviving. These primarily consist of {{ill|Wooden Coffin|lt=Wooden Coffin|lt=Wooden coffin burials|ja|木棺直葬|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/wooden coffin burials|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 古墳}} or large cylindrical haniwa repurposed as coffins, with a single example of a clay sarcophagus. The Shichinotsubo Kofun (Tomb 130) features the foundation of an early corridor-style stone chamber, rare for the cluster<ref name="#1"/>.
Artifacts discovered include iron swords, axes, sickles, Sue pottery, [[:en:Haji pottery|Haji pottery]], beads, and horse fittings. Numerous haniwa have also been unearthed, including cylindrical, jar-shaped, and figurative types (e.g., human, horse, armor, shield, roof, and boat-shaped haniwa). Notably, boat-shaped haniwa from Tombs 1 and 2 of the Takamawari subgroup have been designated Important Cultural Properties by the Japanese government<ref>Kyojima (2007) p.29</ref><ref>Monthly Cultural Properties, 1992, 11.17-20, 42</ref>.
== Designated Cultural Properties == === Important Cultural Properties (National) === * [[:en:Haniwa|Haniwa]] from the Nagahara Takamawari Kofun Group, including: ''' Tomb 1: house-shaped, boat-shaped, cylindrical haniwa, and more. ''' Tomb 2: house-shaped, boat-shaped, shield-shaped, and armor-shaped haniwa.
=== Osaka City Designated Cultural Properties === * Shield-shaped haniwa from Tomb 40. * Burial goods from Shichinotsubo Kofun.
== References == {{Reflist|2}}
== Bibliography == * Nagayama Masakazu, "Nagahara and Kami Kofun Clusters," in *New History of Osaka City, Vol. 1*, 1988. * Takashima Tetsu, "Nagahara Kofun Cluster," in *Comprehensive Dictionary of Japanese Kofun*, Tokyo Publishing, 1989. * Terai Makoto, *Nagahara Ruins Excavation Report XV*, Osaka Cultural Properties Association, 2006. == See Also == * {{ill|Misono Kofun|ja|美園古墳|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Misono Kofun|qq=}}
== External Links == * [http://www.occpa.or.jp/ikou/haniwa_gallery/ikou_01_01.html#gallery_05 Nagahara Takamawari Haniwa Gallery (Osaka Cultural Properties Research Institute)] * [https://www.city.osaka.lg.jp/kyoiku/page/0000074306.html Shichinotsubo Kofun Burial Goods (Osaka City)]

Numerous haniwa have also been unearthed, including cylindrical, jar-shaped, and figurative types (e.g., human, horse, armor, shield, roof, and boat-shaped haniwa). Notably, boat-shaped haniwa from Tombs 1 and 2 of the Takamawari subgroup have been designated Important Cultural Properties by the Japanese government<ref>Kyojima (2007) p.29</ref><ref>Monthly Cultural Properties, 1992, 11.17-20, 42</ref>.
== Designated Cultural Properties == === Important Cultural Properties (National) === * [[:en:Haniwa|Haniwa]] from the Nagahara Takamawari Kofun Group, including: ''' Tomb 1: house-shaped, boat-shaped, cylindrical haniwa, and more. ''' Tomb 2: house-shaped, boat-shaped, shield-shaped, and armor-shaped haniwa.
=== Osaka City Designated Cultural Properties === * Shield-shaped haniwa from Tomb 40. * Burial goods from Shichinotsubo Kofun.
== References == {{Reflist|2}}
== Bibliography == * Nagayama Masakazu, "Nagahara and Kami Kofun Clusters," in *New History of Osaka City, Vol. 1*, 1988. * Takashima Tetsu, "Nagahara Kofun Cluster," in *Comprehensive Dictionary of Japanese Kofun*, Tokyo Publishing, 1989. * Terai Makoto, *Nagahara Ruins Excavation Report XV*, Osaka Cultural Properties Association, 2006. == See Also == * {{ill|Misono Kofun|ja|美園古墳|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Misono Kofun|qq=}}
== External Links == * [http://www.occpa.or.jp/ikou/haniwa_gallery/ikou_01_01.html#gallery_05 Nagahara Takamawari Haniwa Gallery (Osaka Cultural Properties Research Institute)] * [https://www.city.osaka.lg.jp/kyoiku/page/0000074306.html Shichinotsubo Kofun Burial Goods (Osaka City)]

revision.comment: Bot: sync Kofun‑gun infobox from Wikidata (image/location/coords/etc.)
revision.text:
{{Infobox ancient site|coordinates={{Coord|34.608555555555554|N|135.56544444444444|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11651554]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:23, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Nagahara Kofun Cluster''' (長原古墳群) refers to a group of [[late Kofun period]] tumuli located beneath Nagahara and its surrounding areas in [[:en:Hirano-ku, Osaka|Hirano-ku]], [[:en:Osaka|Osaka City]], [[:en:Osaka Prefecture|Osaka Prefecture]]. This cluster, which dates from the late 4th to early 6th centuries, was discovered during the extension of the {{ill|Osaka Municipal Subway Tanimachi Line|ja|大阪市営地下鉄谷町線|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Osaka Municipal Subway Tanimachi Line|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to Osaka Metro谷町線}} and is known for the excavation of over 200 tumuli and numerous [[:en:Haniwa|haniwa]].
== Discovery and Investigation of the Nagahara Kofun Cluster == The discovery of the Nagahara Kofun Cluster occurred in 1973, during an excavation survey prompted by the extension of the Osaka Municipal Subway Tanimachi Line. Although there were no visible surface traces of tumuli, cylindrical haniwa began to emerge, leading to further investigation. Subsequent surveys revealed numerous small tumuli remnants with circumferential moats, despite much of their mound soil having been lost<ref>Nagayama (1988) 11.400-401</ref>.
Among the discoveries was a large circular mound over 50 meters in diameter, with remnants indicating a two-tiered structure. This tumulus, located in an area historically called "Tsukano-moto," was named the Tsukano-moto Kofun. Further examination of cadastral maps uncovered place names such as Ikka-zuka, Ochizuka, Kitsunezuka, and Takazuka, indicating the possible past presence of tumuli. The total area of the cluster spans approximately 2 kilometers north-south and 1.5 kilometers east-west<ref>Nagayama (1988) 11.401-402</ref>.
As a result of construction projects, such as the [[:en:Kinki Expressway|Kinki Expressway]], urban planning initiatives, and residential developments, archaeological surveys continued, identifying 213 tumuli by 2006<ref>Terai (2006) p.273</ref>.
== Formation Period == The Nagahara Kofun Cluster is believed to have formed over four periods, from the late 4th century to the early 6th century, based on the chronology of haniwa and [[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]] unearthed at the site<ref>Nagayama (1988) p.405</ref><ref name="takashima424">Takashima (1989) p.424</ref>. 1. '''Phase 1 (late 4th century)''': Larger tumuli, such as the Tsukano-moto Kofun and Ikka-zuka Kofun (scallop-shaped keyhole tomb), were constructed. 2. '''Phases 2-3 (5th century)''': Smaller tumuli, primarily square-shaped, were built surrounding the earlier large tumuli. 3. '''Phase 4 (early 6th century)''': Tumuli such as the Shichinotsubo Kofun and Minamiguchi Kofun were constructed, marking the end of activity at the site<ref>Nagayama (1988) 11.404-415</ref><ref name="#1">Terai (2006) 11.274-275</ref>.
The predominance of small square tumuli from the 5th century aligns the Nagahara Kofun Cluster with early cluster tumuli like the [[:en:Niizawa Senzuka Kofun Cluster|Niizawa Senzuka Kofun Cluster]] in Nara. Furthermore, its contemporaneity with the [[:en:Furuichi Kofun Cluster|Furuichi Kofun Cluster]], one of Japan's largest kofun clusters, suggests a possible connection. The evolution from larger Phase 1 tumuli to smaller Phase 2-3 tumuli indicates a shift from indirect regional chieftain rule to direct authority by the {{ill|Yamato kingship|ja|大王権|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato kingship|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}<ref name="#2">Ōba (2006) p.1</ref>. Some scholars propose that the buried individuals were primitive bureaucrats integrated into Yamato's administrative system<ref>Miki (2011) p.239</ref>.
== Internal Structure and Artifacts == Due to extensive later flattening, intact burial facilities are rare, with only 18–19 examples surviving. These primarily consist of {{ill|Wooden Coffin|lt=Wooden Coffin|lt=Wooden coffin burials|ja|木棺直葬|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/wooden coffin burials|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 古墳}} or large cylindrical haniwa repurposed as coffins, with a single example of a clay sarcophagus. The Shichinotsubo Kofun (Tomb 130) features the foundation of an early corridor-style stone chamber, rare for the cluster<ref name="#1"/>.
Artifacts discovered include iron swords, axes, sickles, Sue pottery, [[:en:Haji pottery|Haji pottery]], beads, and horse fittings. Numerous haniwa have also been unearthed, including cylindrical, jar-shaped, and figurative types (e.g., human, horse, armor, shield, roof, and boat-shaped haniwa). Notably, boat-shaped haniwa from Tombs 1 and 2 of the Takamawari subgroup have been designated Important Cultural Properties by the Japanese government<ref>Kyojima (2007) p.29</ref><ref>Monthly Cultural Properties, 1992, 11.17-20, 42</ref>.
== Designated Cultural Properties == === Important Cultural Properties (National) === * [[:en:Haniwa|Haniwa]] from the Nagahara Takamawari Kofun Group, including: ''' Tomb 1: house-shaped, boat-shaped, cylindrical haniwa, and more. ''' Tomb 2: house-shaped, boat-shaped, shield-shaped, and armor-shaped haniwa.
=== Osaka City Designated Cultural Properties === * Shield-shaped haniwa from Tomb 40. * Burial goods from Shichinotsubo Kofun.
== References == {{Reflist|2}}
== Bibliography == * Nagayama Masakazu, "Nagahara and Kami Kofun Clusters," in *New History of Osaka City, Vol. 1*, 1988. * Takashima Tetsu, "Nagahara Kofun Cluster," in *Comprehensive Dictionary of Japanese Kofun*, Tokyo Publishing, 1989. * Terai Makoto, *Nagahara Ruins Excavation Report XV*, Osaka Cultural Properties Association, 2006. == See Also == * {{ill|Misono Kofun|ja|美園古墳|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Misono Kofun|qq=}}
== External Links == * [http://www.occpa.or.jp/ikou/haniwa_gallery/ikou_01_01.html#gallery_05 Nagahara Takamawari Haniwa Gallery (Osaka Cultural Properties Research Institute)] * [https://www.city.osaka.lg.jp/kyoiku/page/0000074306.html Shichinotsubo Kofun Burial Goods (Osaka City)]

As a result of construction projects, such as the [[:en:Kinki Expressway|Kinki Expressway]], urban planning initiatives, and residential developments, archaeological surveys continued, identifying 213 tumuli by 2006<ref>Terai (2006) p.273</ref>.
== Formation Period == The Nagahara Kofun Cluster is believed to have formed over four periods, from the late 4th century to the early 6th century, based on the chronology of haniwa and [[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]] unearthed at the site<ref>Nagayama (1988) p.405</ref><ref name="takashima424">Takashima (1989) p.424</ref>. 1. '''Phase 1 (late 4th century)''': Larger tumuli, such as the Tsukano-moto Kofun and Ikka-zuka Kofun (scallop-shaped keyhole tomb), were constructed. 2. '''Phases 2-3 (5th century)''': Smaller tumuli, primarily square-shaped, were built surrounding the earlier large tumuli. 3. '''Phase 4 (early 6th century)''': Tumuli such as the Shichinotsubo Kofun and Minamiguchi Kofun were constructed, marking the end of activity at the site<ref>Nagayama (1988) 11.404-415</ref><ref name="#1">Terai (2006) 11.274-275</ref>.
The predominance of small square tumuli from the 5th century aligns the Nagahara Kofun Cluster with early cluster tumuli like the [[:en:Niizawa Senzuka Kofun Cluster|Niizawa Senzuka Kofun Cluster]] in Nara. Furthermore, its contemporaneity with the [[:en:Furuichi Kofun Cluster|Furuichi Kofun Cluster]], one of Japan's largest kofun clusters, suggests a possible connection. The evolution from larger Phase 1 tumuli to smaller Phase 2-3 tumuli indicates a shift from indirect regional chieftain rule to direct authority by the {{ill|Yamato kingship|ja|大王権|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato kingship|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}<ref name="#2">Ōba (2006) p.1</ref>. Some scholars propose that the buried individuals were primitive bureaucrats integrated into Yamato's administrative system<ref>Miki (2011) p.239</ref>.
== Internal Structure and Artifacts == Due to extensive later flattening, intact burial facilities are rare, with only 18–19 examples surviving. These primarily consist of {{ill|Wooden Coffin|lt=Wooden Coffin|lt=Wooden coffin burials|ja|木棺直葬|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/wooden coffin burials|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 古墳}} or large cylindrical haniwa repurposed as coffins, with a single example of a clay sarcophagus. The Shichinotsubo Kofun (Tomb 130) features the foundation of an early corridor-style stone chamber, rare for the cluster<ref name="#1"/>.
Artifacts discovered include iron swords, axes, sickles, Sue pottery, [[:en:Haji pottery|Haji pottery]], beads, and horse fittings. Numerous haniwa have also been unearthed, including cylindrical, jar-shaped, and figurative types (e.g., human, horse, armor, shield, roof, and boat-shaped haniwa). Notably, boat-shaped haniwa from Tombs 1 and 2 of the Takamawari subgroup have been designated Important Cultural Properties by the Japanese government<ref>Kyojima (2007) p.29</ref><ref>Monthly Cultural Properties, 1992, 11.17-20, 42</ref>.
== Designated Cultural Properties == === Important Cultural Properties (National) === * [[:en:Haniwa|Haniwa]] from the Nagahara Takamawari Kofun Group, including: ''' Tomb 1: house-shaped, boat-shaped, cylindrical haniwa, and more. ''' Tomb 2: house-shaped, boat-shaped, shield-shaped, and armor-shaped haniwa.
=== Osaka City Designated Cultural Properties === * Shield-shaped haniwa from Tomb 40. * Burial goods from Shichinotsubo Kofun.

revision.comment: Text replacement - "pages without english wikidata name" to "qq"
revision.text:
{{Infobox ancient site|coordinates={{Coord|34.608555555555554|N|135.56544444444444|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11651554]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:23, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Nagahara Kofun Cluster''' (長原古墳群) refers to a group of [[late Kofun period]] tumuli located beneath Nagahara and its surrounding areas in [[:en:Hirano-ku, Osaka|Hirano-ku]], [[:en:Osaka|Osaka City]], [[:en:Osaka Prefecture|Osaka Prefecture]]. This cluster, which dates from the late 4th to early 6th centuries, was discovered during the extension of the {{ill|Osaka Municipal Subway Tanimachi Line|ja|大阪市営地下鉄谷町線|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Osaka Municipal Subway Tanimachi Line|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to Osaka Metro谷町線|1=Tanimachi Line|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1142773}} and is known for the excavation of over 200 tumuli and numerous [[:en:Haniwa|haniwa]].
== Discovery and Investigation of the Nagahara Kofun Cluster == The discovery of the Nagahara Kofun Cluster occurred in 1973, during an excavation survey prompted by the extension of the Osaka Municipal Subway Tanimachi Line. Although there were no visible surface traces of tumuli, cylindrical haniwa began to emerge, leading to further investigation. Subsequent surveys revealed numerous small tumuli remnants with circumferential moats, despite much of their mound soil having been lost<ref>Nagayama (1988) 11.400-401</ref>.
Among the discoveries was a large circular mound over 50 meters in diameter, with remnants indicating a two-tiered structure. This tumulus, located in an area historically called "Tsukano-moto," was named the Tsukano-moto Kofun. Further examination of cadastral maps uncovered place names such as Ikka-zuka, Ochizuka, Kitsunezuka, and Takazuka, indicating the possible past presence of tumuli. The total area of the cluster spans approximately 2 kilometers north-south and 1.5 kilometers east-west<ref>Nagayama (1988) 11.401-402</ref>.
As a result of construction projects, such as the [[:en:Kinki Expressway|Kinki Expressway]], urban planning initiatives, and residential developments, archaeological surveys continued, identifying 213 tumuli by 2006<ref>Terai (2006) p.273</ref>.
== Formation Period == The Nagahara Kofun Cluster is believed to have formed over four periods, from the late 4th century to the early 6th century, based on the chronology of haniwa and [[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]] unearthed at the site<ref>Nagayama (1988) p.405</ref><ref name="takashima424">Takashima (1989) p.424</ref>. 1. '''Phase 1 (late 4th century)''': Larger tumuli, such as the Tsukano-moto Kofun and Ikka-zuka Kofun (scallop-shaped keyhole tomb), were constructed. 2. '''Phases 2-3 (5th century)''': Smaller tumuli, primarily square-shaped, were built surrounding the earlier large tumuli. 3. '''Phase 4 (early 6th century)''': Tumuli such as the Shichinotsubo Kofun and Minamiguchi Kofun were constructed, marking the end of activity at the site<ref>Nagayama (1988) 11.404-415</ref><ref name="#1">Terai (2006) 11.274-275</ref>.
The predominance of small square tumuli from the 5th century aligns the Nagahara Kofun Cluster with early cluster tumuli like the [[:en:Niizawa Senzuka Kofun Cluster|Niizawa Senzuka Kofun Cluster]] in Nara. Furthermore, its contemporaneity with the [[:en:Furuichi Kofun Cluster|Furuichi Kofun Cluster]], one of Japan's largest kofun clusters, suggests a possible connection. The evolution from larger Phase 1 tumuli to smaller Phase 2-3 tumuli indicates a shift from indirect regional chieftain rule to direct authority by the {{ill|Yamato kingship|ja|大王権|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato kingship|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}<ref name="#2">Ōba (2006) p.1</ref>. Some scholars propose that the buried individuals were primitive bureaucrats integrated into Yamato's administrative system<ref>Miki (2011) p.239</ref>.
== Internal Structure and Artifacts == Due to extensive later flattening, intact burial facilities are rare, with only 18–19 examples surviving. These primarily consist of {{ill|Wooden Coffin|lt=Wooden Coffin|lt=Wooden coffin burials|ja|木棺直葬|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/wooden coffin burials|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 古墳}} or large cylindrical haniwa repurposed as coffins, with a single example of a clay sarcophagus. The Shichinotsubo Kofun (Tomb 130) features the foundation of an early corridor-style stone chamber, rare for the cluster<ref name="#1"/>.
Artifacts discovered include iron swords, axes, sickles, Sue pottery, [[:en:Haji pottery|Haji pottery]], beads, and horse fittings. Numerous haniwa have also been unearthed, including cylindrical, jar-shaped, and figurative types (e.g., human, horse, armor, shield, roof, and boat-shaped haniwa). Notably, boat-shaped haniwa from Tombs 1 and 2 of the Takamawari subgroup have been designated Important Cultural Properties by the Japanese government<ref>Kyojima (2007) p.29</ref><ref>Monthly Cultural Properties, 1992, 11.17-20, 42</ref>.
== Designated Cultural Properties == === Important Cultural Properties (National) === * [[:en:Haniwa|Haniwa]] from the Nagahara Takamawari Kofun Group, including: ''' Tomb 1: house-shaped, boat-shaped, cylindrical haniwa, and more. ''' Tomb 2: house-shaped, boat-shaped, shield-shaped, and armor-shaped haniwa.
=== Osaka City Designated Cultural Properties === * Shield-shaped haniwa from Tomb 40. * Burial goods from Shichinotsubo Kofun.
== References == {{Reflist|2}}
== Bibliography == * Nagayama Masakazu, "Nagahara and Kami Kofun Clusters," in *New History of Osaka City, Vol. 1*, 1988. * Takashima Tetsu, "Nagahara Kofun Cluster," in *Comprehensive Dictionary of Japanese Kofun*, Tokyo Publishing, 1989. * Terai Makoto, *Nagahara Ruins Excavation Report XV*, Osaka Cultural Properties Association, 2006. == See Also == * {{ill|Misono Kofun|ja|美園古墳|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Misono Kofun|qq=}}
== External Links == * [http://www.occpa.or.jp/ikou/haniwa_gallery/ikou_01_01.html#gallery_05 Nagahara Takamawari Haniwa Gallery (Osaka Cultural Properties Research Institute)] * [https://www.city.osaka.lg.jp/kyoiku/page/0000074306.html Shichinotsubo Kofun Burial Goods (Osaka City)]

These primarily consist of {{ill|Wooden Coffin|lt=Wooden Coffin|lt=Wooden coffin burials|ja|木棺直葬|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/wooden coffin burials|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 古墳}} or large cylindrical haniwa repurposed as coffins, with a single example of a clay sarcophagus. The Shichinotsubo Kofun (Tomb 130) features the foundation of an early corridor-style stone chamber, rare for the cluster<ref name="#1"/>.
Artifacts discovered include iron swords, axes, sickles, Sue pottery, [[:en:Haji pottery|Haji pottery]], beads, and horse fittings. Numerous haniwa have also been unearthed, including cylindrical, jar-shaped, and figurative types (e.g., human, horse, armor, shield, roof, and boat-shaped haniwa). Notably, boat-shaped haniwa from Tombs 1 and 2 of the Takamawari subgroup have been designated Important Cultural Properties by the Japanese government<ref>Kyojima (2007) p.29</ref><ref>Monthly Cultural Properties, 1992, 11.17-20, 42</ref>.
== Designated Cultural Properties == === Important Cultural Properties (National) === * [[:en:Haniwa|Haniwa]] from the Nagahara Takamawari Kofun Group, including: ''' Tomb 1: house-shaped, boat-shaped, cylindrical haniwa, and more. ''' Tomb 2: house-shaped, boat-shaped, shield-shaped, and armor-shaped haniwa.
=== Osaka City Designated Cultural Properties === * Shield-shaped haniwa from Tomb 40. * Burial goods from Shichinotsubo Kofun.
== References == {{Reflist|2}}
== Bibliography == * Nagayama Masakazu, "Nagahara and Kami Kofun Clusters," in *New History of Osaka City, Vol. 1*, 1988. * Takashima Tetsu, "Nagahara Kofun Cluster," in *Comprehensive Dictionary of Japanese Kofun*, Tokyo Publishing, 1989. * Terai Makoto, *Nagahara Ruins Excavation Report XV*, Osaka Cultural Properties Association, 2006. == See Also == * {{ill|Misono Kofun|ja|美園古墳|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Misono Kofun|qq=}}
== External Links == * [http://www.occpa.or.jp/ikou/haniwa_gallery/ikou_01_01.html#gallery_05 Nagahara Takamawari Haniwa Gallery (Osaka Cultural Properties Research Institute)] * [https://www.city.osaka.lg.jp/kyoiku/page/0000074306.html Shichinotsubo Kofun Burial Goods (Osaka City)]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:長原古墳群]] {{Infobox ancient site|coordinates={{Coord|34.608555555555554|N|135.56544444444444|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11651554]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:23, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Nagahara Kofun Cluster''' (長原古墳群) refers to a group of [[late Kofun period]] tumuli located beneath Nagahara and its surrounding areas in [[:en:Hirano-ku, Osaka|Hirano-ku]], [[:en:Osaka|Osaka City]], [[:en:Osaka Prefecture|Osaka Prefecture]]. This cluster, which dates from the late 4th to early 6th centuries, was discovered during the extension of the {{ill|Osaka Municipal Subway Tanimachi Line|ja|大阪市営地下鉄谷町線|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Osaka Municipal Subway Tanimachi Line|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to Osaka Metro谷町線|1=Tanimachi Line|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1142773}} and is known for the excavation of over 200 tumuli and numerous [[:en:Haniwa|haniwa]].
== Discovery and Investigation of the Nagahara Kofun Cluster == The discovery of the Nagahara Kofun Cluster occurred in 1973, during an excavation survey prompted by the extension of the Osaka Municipal Subway Tanimachi Line. Although there were no visible surface traces of tumuli, cylindrical haniwa began to emerge, leading to further investigation. Subsequent surveys revealed numerous small tumuli remnants with circumferential moats, despite much of their mound soil having been lost<ref>Nagayama (1988) 11.400-401</ref>.
Among the discoveries was a large circular mound over 50 meters in diameter, with remnants indicating a two-tiered structure. This tumulus, located in an area historically called "Tsukano-moto," was named the Tsukano-moto Kofun. Further examination of cadastral maps uncovered place names such as Ikka-zuka, Ochizuka, Kitsunezuka, and Takazuka, indicating the possible past presence of tumuli. The total area of the cluster spans approximately 2 kilometers north-south and 1.5 kilometers east-west<ref>Nagayama (1988) 11.401-402</ref>.
As a result of construction projects, such as the [[:en:Kinki Expressway|Kinki Expressway]], urban planning initiatives, and residential developments, archaeological surveys continued, identifying 213 tumuli by 2006<ref>Terai (2006) p.273</ref>.
== Formation Period == The Nagahara Kofun Cluster is believed to have formed over four periods, from the late 4th century to the early 6th century, based on the chronology of haniwa and [[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]] unearthed at the site<ref>Nagayama (1988) p.405</ref><ref name="takashima424">Takashima (1989) p.424</ref>. 1. '''Phase 1 (late 4th century)''': Larger tumuli, such as the Tsukano-moto Kofun and Ikka-zuka Kofun (scallop-shaped keyhole tomb), were constructed. 2. '''Phases 2-3 (5th century)''': Smaller tumuli, primarily square-shaped, were built surrounding the earlier large tumuli. 3. '''Phase 4 (early 6th century)''': Tumuli such as the Shichinotsubo Kofun and Minamiguchi Kofun were constructed, marking the end of activity at the site<ref>Nagayama (1988) 11.404-415</ref><ref name="#1">Terai (2006) 11.274-275</ref>.
The predominance of small square tumuli from the 5th century aligns the Nagahara Kofun Cluster with early cluster tumuli like the [[:en:Niizawa Senzuka Kofun Cluster|Niizawa Senzuka Kofun Cluster]] in Nara. Furthermore, its contemporaneity with the [[:en:Furuichi Kofun Cluster|Furuichi Kofun Cluster]], one of Japan's largest kofun clusters, suggests a possible connection. The evolution from larger Phase 1 tumuli to smaller Phase 2-3 tumuli indicates a shift from indirect regional chieftain rule to direct authority by the {{ill|Yamato kingship|ja|大王権|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato kingship|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}<ref name="#2">Ōba (2006) p.1</ref>. Some scholars propose that the buried individuals were primitive bureaucrats integrated into Yamato's administrative system<ref>Miki (2011) p.239</ref>.
== Internal Structure and Artifacts == Due to extensive later flattening, intact burial facilities are rare, with only 18–19 examples surviving. These primarily consist of {{ill|Wooden Coffin|lt=Wooden Coffin|lt=Wooden coffin burials|ja|木棺直葬|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/wooden coffin burials|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 古墳}} or large cylindrical haniwa repurposed as coffins, with a single example of a clay sarcophagus. The Shichinotsubo Kofun (Tomb 130) features the foundation of an early corridor-style stone chamber, rare for the cluster<ref name="#1"/>.
Artifacts discovered include iron swords, axes, sickles, Sue pottery, [[:en:Haji pottery|Haji pottery]], beads, and horse fittings. Numerous haniwa have also been unearthed, including cylindrical, jar-shaped, and figurative types (e.g., human, horse, armor, shield, roof, and boat-shaped haniwa). Notably, boat-shaped haniwa from Tombs 1 and 2 of the Takamawari subgroup have been designated Important Cultural Properties by the Japanese government<ref>Kyojima (2007) p.29</ref><ref>Monthly Cultural Properties, 1992, 11.17-20, 42</ref>.
== Designated Cultural Properties == === Important Cultural Properties (National) === * [[:en:Haniwa|Haniwa]] from the Nagahara Takamawari Kofun Group, including: ''' Tomb 1: house-shaped, boat-shaped, cylindrical haniwa, and more. ''' Tomb 2: house-shaped, boat-shaped, shield-shaped, and armor-shaped haniwa.
=== Osaka City Designated Cultural Properties === * Shield-shaped haniwa from Tomb 40. * Burial goods from Shichinotsubo Kofun.
== References == {{Reflist|2}}
== Bibliography == * Nagayama Masakazu, "Nagahara and Kami Kofun Clusters," in *New History of Osaka City, Vol. 1*, 1988. * Takashima Tetsu, "Nagahara Kofun Cluster," in *Comprehensive Dictionary of Japanese Kofun*, Tokyo Publishing, 1989. * Terai Makoto, *Nagahara Ruins Excavation Report XV*, Osaka Cultural Properties Association, 2006. == See Also == * {{ill|Misono Kofun|ja|美園古墳|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Misono Kofun|qq=}}
== External Links == * [http://www.occpa.or.jp/ikou/haniwa_gallery/ikou_01_01.html#gallery_05 Nagahara Takamawari Haniwa Gallery (Osaka Cultural Properties Research Institute)] * [https://www.city.osaka.lg.jp/kyoiku/page/0000074306.html Shichinotsubo Kofun Burial Goods (Osaka City)]

Subsequent surveys revealed numerous small tumuli remnants with circumferential moats, despite much of their mound soil having been lost<ref>Nagayama (1988) 11.400-401</ref>.
Among the discoveries was a large circular mound over 50 meters in diameter, with remnants indicating a two-tiered structure. This tumulus, located in an area historically called "Tsukano-moto," was named the Tsukano-moto Kofun. Further examination of cadastral maps uncovered place names such as Ikka-zuka, Ochizuka, Kitsunezuka, and Takazuka, indicating the possible past presence of tumuli. The total area of the cluster spans approximately 2 kilometers north-south and 1.5 kilometers east-west<ref>Nagayama (1988) 11.401-402</ref>.
As a result of construction projects, such as the [[:en:Kinki Expressway|Kinki Expressway]], urban planning initiatives, and residential developments, archaeological surveys continued, identifying 213 tumuli by 2006<ref>Terai (2006) p.273</ref>.
== Formation Period == The Nagahara Kofun Cluster is believed to have formed over four periods, from the late 4th century to the early 6th century, based on the chronology of haniwa and [[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]] unearthed at the site<ref>Nagayama (1988) p.405</ref><ref name="takashima424">Takashima (1989) p.424</ref>. 1. '''Phase 1 (late 4th century)''': Larger tumuli, such as the Tsukano-moto Kofun and Ikka-zuka Kofun (scallop-shaped keyhole tomb), were constructed. 2. '''Phases 2-3 (5th century)''': Smaller tumuli, primarily square-shaped, were built surrounding the earlier large tumuli. 3. '''Phase 4 (early 6th century)''': Tumuli such as the Shichinotsubo Kofun and Minamiguchi Kofun were constructed, marking the end of activity at the site<ref>Nagayama (1988) 11.404-415</ref><ref name="#1">Terai (2006) 11.274-275</ref>.
The predominance of small square tumuli from the 5th century aligns the Nagahara Kofun Cluster with early cluster tumuli like the [[:en:Niizawa Senzuka Kofun Cluster|Niizawa Senzuka Kofun Cluster]] in Nara. Furthermore, its contemporaneity with the [[:en:Furuichi Kofun Cluster|Furuichi Kofun Cluster]], one of Japan's largest kofun clusters, suggests a possible connection. The evolution from larger Phase 1 tumuli to smaller Phase 2-3 tumuli indicates a shift from indirect regional chieftain rule to direct authority by the {{ill|Yamato kingship|ja|大王権|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato kingship|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}<ref name="#2">Ōba (2006) p.1</ref>. Some scholars propose that the buried individuals were primitive bureaucrats integrated into Yamato's administrative system<ref>Miki (2011) p.239</ref>.
== Internal Structure and Artifacts == Due to extensive later flattening, intact burial facilities are rare, with only 18–19 examples surviving. These primarily consist of {{ill|Wooden Coffin|lt=Wooden Coffin|lt=Wooden coffin burials|ja|木棺直葬|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/wooden coffin burials|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 古墳}} or large cylindrical haniwa repurposed as coffins, with a single example of a clay sarcophagus. The Shichinotsubo Kofun (Tomb 130) features the foundation of an early corridor-style stone chamber, rare for the cluster<ref name="#1"/>.
Artifacts discovered include iron swords, axes, sickles, Sue pottery, [[:en:Haji pottery|Haji pottery]], beads, and horse fittings. Numerous haniwa have also been unearthed, including cylindrical, jar-shaped, and figurative types (e.g., human, horse, armor, shield, roof, and boat-shaped haniwa). Notably, boat-shaped haniwa from Tombs 1 and 2 of the Takamawari subgroup have been designated Important Cultural Properties by the Japanese government<ref>Kyojima (2007) p.29</ref><ref>Monthly Cultural Properties, 1992, 11.17-20, 42</ref>.
== Designated Cultural Properties == === Important Cultural Properties (National) === * [[:en:Haniwa|Haniwa]] from the Nagahara Takamawari Kofun Group, including: ''' Tomb 1: house-shaped, boat-shaped, cylindrical haniwa, and more. ''' Tomb 2: house-shaped, boat-shaped, shield-shaped, and armor-shaped haniwa.
=== Osaka City Designated Cultural Properties === * Shield-shaped haniwa from Tomb 40. * Burial goods from Shichinotsubo Kofun.
== References == {{Reflist|2}}
== Bibliography == * Nagayama Masakazu, "Nagahara and Kami Kofun Clusters," in *New History of Osaka City, Vol. 1*, 1988. * Takashima Tetsu, "Nagahara Kofun Cluster," in *Comprehensive Dictionary of Japanese Kofun*, Tokyo Publishing, 1989. * Terai Makoto, *Nagahara Ruins Excavation Report XV*, Osaka Cultural Properties Association, 2006. == See Also == * {{ill|Misono Kofun|ja|美園古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Misono Kofun|qq=}}
== External Links == * [http://www.occpa.or.jp/ikou/haniwa_gallery/ikou_01_01.html#gallery_05 Nagahara Takamawari Haniwa Gallery (Osaka Cultural Properties Research Institute)] * [https://www.city.osaka.lg.jp/kyoiku/page/0000074306.html Shichinotsubo Kofun Burial Goods (Osaka City)]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:長原古墳群]] {{Infobox ancient site|coordinates={{Coord|34.608555555555554|N|135.56544444444444|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11651554]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:23, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Nagahara Kofun Cluster''' (長原古墳群) refers to a group of [[late Kofun period]] tumuli located beneath Nagahara and its surrounding areas in [[:en:Hirano-ku, Osaka|Hirano-ku]], [[:en:Osaka|Osaka City]], [[:en:Osaka Prefecture|Osaka Prefecture]]. This cluster, which dates from the late 4th to early 6th centuries, was discovered during the extension of the {{ill|Osaka Municipal Subway Tanimachi Line|ja|大阪市営地下鉄谷町線|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Osaka Municipal Subway Tanimachi Line|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to Osaka Metro谷町線|1=Tanimachi Line|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1142773}} and is known for the excavation of over 200 tumuli and numerous [[:en:Haniwa|haniwa]].
== Discovery and Investigation of the Nagahara Kofun Cluster == The discovery of the Nagahara Kofun Cluster occurred in 1973, during an excavation survey prompted by the extension of the Osaka Municipal Subway Tanimachi Line. Although there were no visible surface traces of tumuli, cylindrical haniwa began to emerge, leading to further investigation. Subsequent surveys revealed numerous small tumuli remnants with circumferential moats, despite much of their mound soil having been lost<ref>Nagayama (1988) 11.400-401</ref>.
Among the discoveries was a large circular mound over 50 meters in diameter, with remnants indicating a two-tiered structure. This tumulus, located in an area historically called "Tsukano-moto," was named the Tsukano-moto Kofun. Further examination of cadastral maps uncovered place names such as Ikka-zuka, Ochizuka, Kitsunezuka, and Takazuka, indicating the possible past presence of tumuli. The total area of the cluster spans approximately 2 kilometers north-south and 1.5 kilometers east-west<ref>Nagayama (1988) 11.401-402</ref>.
As a result of construction projects, such as the [[:en:Kinki Expressway|Kinki Expressway]], urban planning initiatives, and residential developments, archaeological surveys continued, identifying 213 tumuli by 2006<ref>Terai (2006) p.273</ref>.
== Formation Period == The Nagahara Kofun Cluster is believed to have formed over four periods, from the late 4th century to the early 6th century, based on the chronology of haniwa and [[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]] unearthed at the site<ref>Nagayama (1988) p.405</ref><ref name="takashima424">Takashima (1989) p.424</ref>. 1. '''Phase 1 (late 4th century)''': Larger tumuli, such as the Tsukano-moto Kofun and Ikka-zuka Kofun (scallop-shaped keyhole tomb), were constructed. 2. '''Phases 2-3 (5th century)''': Smaller tumuli, primarily square-shaped, were built surrounding the earlier large tumuli. 3. '''Phase 4 (early 6th century)''': Tumuli such as the Shichinotsubo Kofun and Minamiguchi Kofun were constructed, marking the end of activity at the site<ref>Nagayama (1988) 11.404-415</ref><ref name="#1">Terai (2006) 11.274-275</ref>.
The predominance of small square tumuli from the 5th century aligns the Nagahara Kofun Cluster with early cluster tumuli like the [[:en:Niizawa Senzuka Kofun Cluster|Niizawa Senzuka Kofun Cluster]] in Nara. Furthermore, its contemporaneity with the [[:en:Furuichi Kofun Cluster|Furuichi Kofun Cluster]], one of Japan's largest kofun clusters, suggests a possible connection. The evolution from larger Phase 1 tumuli to smaller Phase 2-3 tumuli indicates a shift from indirect regional chieftain rule to direct authority by the {{ill|Yamato kingship|ja|大王権|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato kingship|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}<ref name="#2">Ōba (2006) p.1</ref>. Some scholars propose that the buried individuals were primitive bureaucrats integrated into Yamato's administrative system<ref>Miki (2011) p.239</ref>.
== Internal Structure and Artifacts == Due to extensive later flattening, intact burial facilities are rare, with only 18–19 examples surviving. These primarily consist of {{ill|Wooden Coffin|lt=Wooden Coffin|lt=Wooden coffin burials|ja|木棺直葬|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/wooden coffin burials|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 古墳}} or large cylindrical haniwa repurposed as coffins, with a single example of a clay sarcophagus. The Shichinotsubo Kofun (Tomb 130) features the foundation of an early corridor-style stone chamber, rare for the cluster<ref name="#1"/>.
Artifacts discovered include iron swords, axes, sickles, Sue pottery, [[:en:Haji pottery|Haji pottery]], beads, and horse fittings. Numerous haniwa have also been unearthed, including cylindrical, jar-shaped, and figurative types (e.g., human, horse, armor, shield, roof, and boat-shaped haniwa). Notably, boat-shaped haniwa from Tombs 1 and 2 of the Takamawari subgroup have been designated Important Cultural Properties by the Japanese government<ref>Kyojima (2007) p.29</ref><ref>Monthly Cultural Properties, 1992, 11.17-20, 42</ref>.
== Designated Cultural Properties == === Important Cultural Properties (National) === * [[:en:Haniwa|Haniwa]] from the Nagahara Takamawari Kofun Group, including: ''' Tomb 1: house-shaped, boat-shaped, cylindrical haniwa, and more. ''' Tomb 2: house-shaped, boat-shaped, shield-shaped, and armor-shaped haniwa.
=== Osaka City Designated Cultural Properties === * Shield-shaped haniwa from Tomb 40. * Burial goods from Shichinotsubo Kofun.
== References == {{Reflist|2}}
== Bibliography == * Nagayama Masakazu, "Nagahara and Kami Kofun Clusters," in *New History of Osaka City, Vol. 1*, 1988. * Takashima Tetsu, "Nagahara Kofun Cluster," in *Comprehensive Dictionary of Japanese Kofun*, Tokyo Publishing, 1989. * Terai Makoto, *Nagahara Ruins Excavation Report XV*, Osaka Cultural Properties Association, 2006. == See Also == * {{ill|Misono Kofun|ja|美園古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Misono Kofun|qq=}}
== External Links == * [http://www.occpa.or.jp/ikou/haniwa_gallery/ikou_01_01.html#gallery_05 Nagahara Takamawari Haniwa Gallery (Osaka Cultural Properties Research Institute)] * [https://www.city.osaka.lg.jp/kyoiku/page/0000074306.html Shichinotsubo Kofun Burial Goods (Osaka City)]

These primarily consist of {{ill|Wooden Coffin|lt=Wooden Coffin|lt=Wooden coffin burials|ja|木棺直葬|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/wooden coffin burials|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 古墳}} or large cylindrical haniwa repurposed as coffins, with a single example of a clay sarcophagus. The Shichinotsubo Kofun (Tomb 130) features the foundation of an early corridor-style stone chamber, rare for the cluster<ref name="#1"/>.
Artifacts discovered include iron swords, axes, sickles, Sue pottery, [[:en:Haji pottery|Haji pottery]], beads, and horse fittings. Numerous haniwa have also been unearthed, including cylindrical, jar-shaped, and figurative types (e.g., human, horse, armor, shield, roof, and boat-shaped haniwa). Notably, boat-shaped haniwa from Tombs 1 and 2 of the Takamawari subgroup have been designated Important Cultural Properties by the Japanese government<ref>Kyojima (2007) p.29</ref><ref>Monthly Cultural Properties, 1992, 11.17-20, 42</ref>.
== Designated Cultural Properties == === Important Cultural Properties (National) === * [[:en:Haniwa|Haniwa]] from the Nagahara Takamawari Kofun Group, including: ''' Tomb 1: house-shaped, boat-shaped, cylindrical haniwa, and more. ''' Tomb 2: house-shaped, boat-shaped, shield-shaped, and armor-shaped haniwa.
=== Osaka City Designated Cultural Properties === * Shield-shaped haniwa from Tomb 40. * Burial goods from Shichinotsubo Kofun.
== References == {{Reflist|2}}
== Bibliography == * Nagayama Masakazu, "Nagahara and Kami Kofun Clusters," in *New History of Osaka City, Vol. 1*, 1988. * Takashima Tetsu, "Nagahara Kofun Cluster," in *Comprehensive Dictionary of Japanese Kofun*, Tokyo Publishing, 1989. * Terai Makoto, *Nagahara Ruins Excavation Report XV*, Osaka Cultural Properties Association, 2006. == See Also == * {{ill|Misono Kofun|ja|美園古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Misono Kofun|qq=}}
== External Links == * [http://www.occpa.or.jp/ikou/haniwa_gallery/ikou_01_01.html#gallery_05 Nagahara Takamawari Haniwa Gallery (Osaka Cultural Properties Research Institute)] * [https://www.city.osaka.lg.jp/kyoiku/page/0000074306.html Shichinotsubo Kofun Burial Goods (Osaka City)]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:長原古墳群]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:長原古墳群]] {{Infobox ancient site|coordinates={{Coord|34.608555555555554|N|135.56544444444444|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11651554]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:23, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Nagahara Kofun Cluster''' (長原古墳群) refers to a group of [[late Kofun period]] tumuli located beneath Nagahara and its surrounding areas in [[:en:Hirano-ku, Osaka|Hirano-ku]], [[:en:Osaka|Osaka City]], [[:en:Osaka Prefecture|Osaka Prefecture]]. This cluster, which dates from the late 4th to early 6th centuries, was discovered during the extension of the {{ill|Osaka Municipal Subway Tanimachi Line|ja|大阪市営地下鉄谷町線|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Osaka Municipal Subway Tanimachi Line|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to Osaka Metro谷町線|1=Tanimachi Line|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1142773}} and is known for the excavation of over 200 tumuli and numerous [[:en:Haniwa|haniwa]].
== Discovery and Investigation of the Nagahara Kofun Cluster == The discovery of the Nagahara Kofun Cluster occurred in 1973, during an excavation survey prompted by the extension of the Osaka Municipal Subway Tanimachi Line. Although there were no visible surface traces of tumuli, cylindrical haniwa began to emerge, leading to further investigation. Subsequent surveys revealed numerous small tumuli remnants with circumferential moats, despite much of their mound soil having been lost<ref>Nagayama (1988) 11.400-401</ref>.
Among the discoveries was a large circular mound over 50 meters in diameter, with remnants indicating a two-tiered structure. This tumulus, located in an area historically called "Tsukano-moto," was named the Tsukano-moto Kofun. Further examination of cadastral maps uncovered place names such as Ikka-zuka, Ochizuka, Kitsunezuka, and Takazuka, indicating the possible past presence of tumuli. The total area of the cluster spans approximately 2 kilometers north-south and 1.5 kilometers east-west<ref>Nagayama (1988) 11.401-402</ref>.
As a result of construction projects, such as the [[:en:Kinki Expressway|Kinki Expressway]], urban planning initiatives, and residential developments, archaeological surveys continued, identifying 213 tumuli by 2006<ref>Terai (2006) p.273</ref>.
== Formation Period == The Nagahara Kofun Cluster is believed to have formed over four periods, from the late 4th century to the early 6th century, based on the chronology of haniwa and [[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]] unearthed at the site<ref>Nagayama (1988) p.405</ref><ref name="takashima424">Takashima (1989) p.424</ref>. 1. '''Phase 1 (late 4th century)''': Larger tumuli, such as the Tsukano-moto Kofun and Ikka-zuka Kofun (scallop-shaped keyhole tomb), were constructed. 2. '''Phases 2-3 (5th century)''': Smaller tumuli, primarily square-shaped, were built surrounding the earlier large tumuli. 3. '''Phase 4 (early 6th century)''': Tumuli such as the Shichinotsubo Kofun and Minamiguchi Kofun were constructed, marking the end of activity at the site<ref>Nagayama (1988) 11.404-415</ref><ref name="#1">Terai (2006) 11.274-275</ref>.
The predominance of small square tumuli from the 5th century aligns the Nagahara Kofun Cluster with early cluster tumuli like the [[:en:Niizawa Senzuka Kofun Cluster|Niizawa Senzuka Kofun Cluster]] in Nara. Furthermore, its contemporaneity with the [[:en:Furuichi Kofun Cluster|Furuichi Kofun Cluster]], one of Japan's largest kofun clusters, suggests a possible connection. The evolution from larger Phase 1 tumuli to smaller Phase 2-3 tumuli indicates a shift from indirect regional chieftain rule to direct authority by the {{ill|Yamato kingship|ja|大王権|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato kingship|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}<ref name="#2">Ōba (2006) p.1</ref>. Some scholars propose that the buried individuals were primitive bureaucrats integrated into Yamato's administrative system<ref>Miki (2011) p.239</ref>.
== Internal Structure and Artifacts == Due to extensive later flattening, intact burial facilities are rare, with only 18–19 examples surviving. These primarily consist of {{ill|Wooden Coffin|lt=Wooden Coffin|lt=Wooden coffin burials|ja|木棺直葬|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/wooden coffin burials|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 古墳}} or large cylindrical haniwa repurposed as coffins, with a single example of a clay sarcophagus. The Shichinotsubo Kofun (Tomb 130) features the foundation of an early corridor-style stone chamber, rare for the cluster<ref name="#1"/>.
Artifacts discovered include iron swords, axes, sickles, Sue pottery, [[:en:Haji pottery|Haji pottery]], beads, and horse fittings. Numerous haniwa have also been unearthed, including cylindrical, jar-shaped, and figurative types (e.g., human, horse, armor, shield, roof, and boat-shaped haniwa). Notably, boat-shaped haniwa from Tombs 1 and 2 of the Takamawari subgroup have been designated Important Cultural Properties by the Japanese government<ref>Kyojima (2007) p.29</ref><ref>Monthly Cultural Properties, 1992, 11.17-20, 42</ref>.
== Designated Cultural Properties == === Important Cultural Properties (National) === * [[:en:Haniwa|Haniwa]] from the Nagahara Takamawari Kofun Group, including: ''' Tomb 1: house-shaped, boat-shaped, cylindrical haniwa, and more. ''' Tomb 2: house-shaped, boat-shaped, shield-shaped, and armor-shaped haniwa.
=== Osaka City Designated Cultural Properties === * Shield-shaped haniwa from Tomb 40. * Burial goods from Shichinotsubo Kofun.
== References == {{Reflist|2}}
== Bibliography == * Nagayama Masakazu, "Nagahara and Kami Kofun Clusters," in *New History of Osaka City, Vol. 1*, 1988. * Takashima Tetsu, "Nagahara Kofun Cluster," in *Comprehensive Dictionary of Japanese Kofun*, Tokyo Publishing, 1989. * Terai Makoto, *Nagahara Ruins Excavation Report XV*, Osaka Cultural Properties Association, 2006. == See Also == * {{ill|Misono Kofun|ja|美園古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Misono Kofun|qq=}}
== External Links == * [http://www.occpa.or.jp/ikou/haniwa_gallery/ikou_01_01.html#gallery_05 Nagahara Takamawari Haniwa Gallery (Osaka Cultural Properties Research Institute)] * [https://www.city.osaka.lg.jp/kyoiku/page/0000074306.html Shichinotsubo Kofun Burial Goods (Osaka City)]

This cluster, which dates from the late 4th to early 6th centuries, was discovered during the extension of the {{ill|Osaka Municipal Subway Tanimachi Line|ja|大阪市営地下鉄谷町線|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Osaka Municipal Subway Tanimachi Line|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to Osaka Metro谷町線|1=Tanimachi Line|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1142773}} and is known for the excavation of over 200 tumuli and numerous [[:en:Haniwa|haniwa]].
== Discovery and Investigation of the Nagahara Kofun Cluster == The discovery of the Nagahara Kofun Cluster occurred in 1973, during an excavation survey prompted by the extension of the Osaka Municipal Subway Tanimachi Line. Although there were no visible surface traces of tumuli, cylindrical haniwa began to emerge, leading to further investigation. Subsequent surveys revealed numerous small tumuli remnants with circumferential moats, despite much of their mound soil having been lost<ref>Nagayama (1988) 11.400-401</ref>.
Among the discoveries was a large circular mound over 50 meters in diameter, with remnants indicating a two-tiered structure. This tumulus, located in an area historically called "Tsukano-moto," was named the Tsukano-moto Kofun. Further examination of cadastral maps uncovered place names such as Ikka-zuka, Ochizuka, Kitsunezuka, and Takazuka, indicating the possible past presence of tumuli. The total area of the cluster spans approximately 2 kilometers north-south and 1.5 kilometers east-west<ref>Nagayama (1988) 11.401-402</ref>.
As a result of construction projects, such as the [[:en:Kinki Expressway|Kinki Expressway]], urban planning initiatives, and residential developments, archaeological surveys continued, identifying 213 tumuli by 2006<ref>Terai (2006) p.273</ref>.
== Formation Period == The Nagahara Kofun Cluster is believed to have formed over four periods, from the late 4th century to the early 6th century, based on the chronology of haniwa and [[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]] unearthed at the site<ref>Nagayama (1988) p.405</ref><ref name="takashima424">Takashima (1989) p.424</ref>. 1. '''Phase 1 (late 4th century)''': Larger tumuli, such as the Tsukano-moto Kofun and Ikka-zuka Kofun (scallop-shaped keyhole tomb), were constructed. 2. '''Phases 2-3 (5th century)''': Smaller tumuli, primarily square-shaped, were built surrounding the earlier large tumuli. 3. '''Phase 4 (early 6th century)''': Tumuli such as the Shichinotsubo Kofun and Minamiguchi Kofun were constructed, marking the end of activity at the site<ref>Nagayama (1988) 11.404-415</ref><ref name="#1">Terai (2006) 11.274-275</ref>.
The predominance of small square tumuli from the 5th century aligns the Nagahara Kofun Cluster with early cluster tumuli like the [[:en:Niizawa Senzuka Kofun Cluster|Niizawa Senzuka Kofun Cluster]] in Nara. Furthermore, its contemporaneity with the [[:en:Furuichi Kofun Cluster|Furuichi Kofun Cluster]], one of Japan's largest kofun clusters, suggests a possible connection. The evolution from larger Phase 1 tumuli to smaller Phase 2-3 tumuli indicates a shift from indirect regional chieftain rule to direct authority by the {{ill|Yamato kingship|ja|大王権|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato kingship|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}<ref name="#2">Ōba (2006) p.1</ref>. Some scholars propose that the buried individuals were primitive bureaucrats integrated into Yamato's administrative system<ref>Miki (2011) p.239</ref>.
== Internal Structure and Artifacts == Due to extensive later flattening, intact burial facilities are rare, with only 18–19 examples surviving. These primarily consist of {{ill|Wooden Coffin|lt=Wooden Coffin|lt=Wooden coffin burials|ja|木棺直葬|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/wooden coffin burials|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 古墳}} or large cylindrical haniwa repurposed as coffins, with a single example of a clay sarcophagus. The Shichinotsubo Kofun (Tomb 130) features the foundation of an early corridor-style stone chamber, rare for the cluster<ref name="#1"/>.
Artifacts discovered include iron swords, axes, sickles, Sue pottery, [[:en:Haji pottery|Haji pottery]], beads, and horse fittings. Numerous haniwa have also been unearthed, including cylindrical, jar-shaped, and figurative types (e.g., human, horse, armor, shield, roof, and boat-shaped haniwa). Notably, boat-shaped haniwa from Tombs 1 and 2 of the Takamawari subgroup have been designated Important Cultural Properties by the Japanese government<ref>Kyojima (2007) p.29</ref><ref>Monthly Cultural Properties, 1992, 11.17-20, 42</ref>.
== Designated Cultural Properties == === Important Cultural Properties (National) === * [[:en:Haniwa|Haniwa]] from the Nagahara Takamawari Kofun Group, including: ''' Tomb 1: house-shaped, boat-shaped, cylindrical haniwa, and more. ''' Tomb 2: house-shaped, boat-shaped, shield-shaped, and armor-shaped haniwa.
=== Osaka City Designated Cultural Properties === * Shield-shaped haniwa from Tomb 40. * Burial goods from Shichinotsubo Kofun.
== References == {{Reflist|2}}
== Bibliography == * Nagayama Masakazu, "Nagahara and Kami Kofun Clusters," in *New History of Osaka City, Vol. 1*, 1988. * Takashima Tetsu, "Nagahara Kofun Cluster," in *Comprehensive Dictionary of Japanese Kofun*, Tokyo Publishing, 1989. * Terai Makoto, *Nagahara Ruins Excavation Report XV*, Osaka Cultural Properties Association, 2006. == See Also == * {{ill|Misono Kofun|ja|美園古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Misono Kofun|qq=}}
== External Links == * [http://www.occpa.or.jp/ikou/haniwa_gallery/ikou_01_01.html#gallery_05 Nagahara Takamawari Haniwa Gallery (Osaka Cultural Properties Research Institute)] * [https://www.city.osaka.lg.jp/kyoiku/page/0000074306.html Shichinotsubo Kofun Burial Goods (Osaka City)]
{{translated page|ja|長原古墳群|version=100954068|comment=Imported full ja history}}
